agent1_snippets.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- With Adjacent Stainless Steel Crowns
agent1_snippets.content: When more thanВ 1 crown is required in a quadrant, both teeth should be prepared during the same visit. There should be slightly more than usual reduction on the adjacent proximal surfaces. This makes it easier to place multiple crowns. Before reducing another tooth, the occlusal reduction of one should be completed. Simultaneous reduction of both teeth results in improper reduction. There is a risk of mesiodistal space loss if there are proximal caries on adjacent teeth. To restore carious adjacent teeth withВ stainless steel crowns,В the preparations should be modified to fit the teeth with smaller crowns than normal. Rather than more proximal reduction, further reduction of the buccal and lingual walls is carried out. [6] [10] [23]

agent1_snippets.content: Place the crown linguo-buccally on the prepared tooth, applying pressure in the buccal direction so the crown slips over the buccal surface and into the gingival sulcus. There is friction as the crown slides over the buccal bulge. Crown positioning be difficultВ if the crown is too small or if the buccal bulge is too excessive. Choose a larger crown or reduce the tooth's buccal bulge in this case. After placing the selected crown over the prepared tooth, ask the patient to bite over it. Check for preliminary occlusal and marginal ridge relation. Previously, scratch a mark on the stainless steel crown at the level of the freeВ gingival margin. Scratch or dotted lines indicate the gingival contour and the portion of the crown to be removed. Remove the crown and trim or cut away the additional crown portion gingivally 1 mm below the scratch line with a crown and bridge scissors or, preferably, a large abrasive wheel (вЂњheatless stoneвЂќ) that allows for more precise reduction. Repeat the procedure until the adequate extension of 1 mm beneath the gingival margin is achieved.

agent1_snippets.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Cementation
agent1_snippets.content: A luting cement should be used to secure the crown. Once the crown is seated on the prepared tooth, it should be kept under pressure while the cement hardens. Manufacturers recommend glass ionomer, zinc polycarboxylate, and zinc phosphate cement but fluoride-leaching cement has additional advantages. Due to the clinical advantages, RMGIC is theВ preferred choice. [22] However, research suggests the type of cement used has little effect on retention, with the essential retentive components derived from proper crown contouring and crimping. Operators must pay deliberate attention to the removal of excess cement. This is normally accomplished by running a pointed instrument around the cemented crown's margins and sliding knotted dental floss buccolingually through the contact areas after the cement setting. Excess cement has been shown to affect gingival health. [8] [6]

agent1_snippets.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Stainless steel crowns are the most commonly used restorative option for repairing and preserving the remaining tissue of severely damaged and decayed teeth (see Image. Stainless Steel Crown). They were introduced into pediatric dentistry in 1947, first described by Engel, and then popularised by Humphrey in 1950. Stainless steel crowns have outperformed other materials, such as amalgam and composite, in terms of durability and longevity for more than a half-century. In fact, no restorative material has provided the benefits of low cost, reliability, and durability when interim full-coronal coverage is required. [3] [4]

agent1_snippets.title: Cracked Tooth Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Indirect cuspal coverage in the form of a crown or onlay may also function extremely well as an external splint, prohibiting the flexure of the crack. Guthrie et al observed that most cracks extended into the cervical third of the crown. In their study, they concluded that 25 of the 28 tested cracked teeth became asymptomatic after receiving a full-coverage crown. [30] A full-coverage crown allows for a more equal distribution of occlusal forces across the affected tooth due to the resistant form of the crown. This prevents the crack's flexure and inhibits it from propagating further and causing more damage. [30] However, tooth preparation for full-coverage crowns is invasive and increases the risk of devitalization. [31]
